notes.anchor: the days of the Roman Games
notes.note: The Ludi Romani, the great September games in honour of Jupiter Optimus Maximus; their dates let Cicero fix when he was at Arpinum.

notes.anchor: the fourth day before the Ides of September
notes.note: The Roman calendar counted backwards (inclusively) from the Ides; the fourth day before the September Ides (13th) is 10 September. The translation supplies the modern date in brackets throughout.

Cicero's letter is a detailed building-inspection report on Quintus's country villas near Arpinum; much of the technical vocabulary (colonnade, walking-gallery, vaultings, stuccowork) concerns the construction work he is supervising in his brother's absence.
The sestertius was the standard unit of account; 101,000 was a modest price for a country property. Cicero is settling his brother's estate purchases while Quintus serves with Caesar in Gaul.
The figure is fifty iugera. The iugerum was the Roman unit of land area, about a quarter of a hectare; fifty iugera is roughly twelve hectares of irrigable meadow.

notes.anchor: a servitude imposed on that estate
notes.note: A servitus in Roman law was a permanent encumbrance binding one property for the benefit of another (here, rights over the water); it would transfer with the land and so affect the resale price.
The vilicus was the resident bailiff or steward, usually a slave or freedman, who managed a Roman estate and its labour and accounts on the owner's behalf.
Palliati, men in the Greek cloak (pallium); a faintly mocking phrase for Greek hangers-on, here the garden-staff so wrapped in ivy they look like Greeks peddling it.

An abusive nickname for L. Calpurnius Piso (cos. 58 BC), one of the consuls Cicero blamed for his exile; 'Calventius' sneers at the Insubrian-Gaulish grandfather of this enemy. The 'speech' is one Piso delivered attacking Cicero.

notes.anchor: on behalf of Scaurus and on behalf of Plancius
notes.note: Cicero's defence speeches Pro Scauro and Pro Plancio, both delivered in 54 BC; the men were on trial respectively for extortion and for electoral bribery.
A literary work (a tragedy on the Erigone myth) that Quintus had composed while on campaign and sent home through Caesar's agent Oppius; Cicero awaits it eagerly.

notes.anchor: the temple of Tellus and Catulus's portico
notes.note: Two public monuments at Rome whose upkeep Cicero attends to for Quintus: the temple of the earth-goddess Tellus on the Esquiline and the portico built by Q. Lutatius Catulus (cos. 78 BC).
A flamen was a priest dedicated to a single deity; the prosecutor L. Lentulus is identified by his father's priesthood (probably the flamen Martialis) to distinguish him from the many other Lentuli.

notes.anchor: who has already laid the charge of
notes.note: The charge is maiestas (full: crimen laesae maiestatis), diminishing the majesty of the Roman state --- the nearest Roman equivalent to treason. Gabinius faced it for his unauthorized Egyptian campaign.
The charge de pecuniis repetundis covered a magistrate's illegal enrichment in his province; it was the standard prosecution against returning governors such as Gabinius.

notes.anchor: a coalition of the consular candidates
notes.note: A coitio was a secret electoral pact among candidates to pool support and fix the outcome; the consular elections of 54 BC were paralysed by bribery scandals when Memmius exposed one such bargain.

An embolium was a dramatic interlude or set-piece; Cicero plans one for the second book of his autobiographical poem De Temporibus Suis ("My Times"), with Apollo before the gods deriding the two disgraced returning generals, Gabinius and Piso.
Imperator was the title of a victorious commander acclaimed by his troops; Cicero applies it sarcastically to Gabinius (who 'sold' his army) and Piso (who 'lost' his).
